reasoning: The Lean signature matches the informal statement component-by-component: (a) the index set `Fin 1066` corresponds to $\{0,1,\ldots,1065\}$ (1066 values); (b) `X : Finset T` with `X.card ≥ 10` captures "finite set with at least 10 elements"; (c) `A i ⊆ X ∧ (A i).card > ((1:ℚ)/2) * X.card` faithfully encodes $A_i \subseteq X$ and $|A_i| > |X|/2$ (strict rational inequality equivalent to $2|A_i|>|X|$); (d) the conclusion `∃ Y, Y ⊆ X ∧ Y.card = 10 ∧ ∀ i, ∃ y ∈ Y, y ∈ A i` captures "there exist 10 elements in X such that each $A_i$ contains at least one of them". Requiring `Y.card = 10` (exactly 10 distinct elements) is the standard reading of "10 elements $x_1,\ldots,x_{10}$" in hitting-set problems, and since $|X| \geq 10$ it is equivalent to "at most 10" (pad with extra elements). No hypothesis is dropped or added beyond what the informal statement requires.
